summary: American conceptual photographer (born 1954) best known for the Untitled Film Stills series — black-and-white self-portraits posing as unnamed women from stereotypical 1950s–60s B-movies. Also created the Centerfolds, Sex Pictures (using medical dolls in explicit positions), History Portraits (imitating Old Masters), and Clowns series. Associated with the Pictures Generation. Chuck Close called her 'perhaps the most important artist of the 1980s.' Laura Mulvey analyzed her work in 'A Phantasmagoria of the Female Body.'

works.name: Untitled Film Stills
works.description: Sherman's best-known series (1977–1980): 69 black-and-white self-portraits posing as unnamed women in stereotypical female roles evocative of 1950s–60s Hollywood B-movies ('City Girl,' 'The Hitchhiker,' housewife, career girl, etc.). Every image is untitled. Includes Untitled #96 (Sherman lying on a kitchen floor in orange, clutching a newspaper ad) and Untitled #92. James Franco controversially tried to reshoot the series with himself. Chuck Close called her 'perhaps the most important artist of the 1980s.' Suzy Lake is cited as a mutual influence. Wikipedia

works.name: Centerfolds series
works.description: Sherman's 1981 series of horizontal-format color photographs imitating magazine centerfolds, but subversively depicting women in distressed or pensive poses — not seductive. Commissioned by Artforum. Includes an image of Sherman clutching a newspaper ad and lying on a floor in an orange sweater. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Horizontal-format color photographs imitating centerfolds but depicting women in distressed/pensive poses; commissioned by Artforum
examples: This artist clutches a newspaper ad and lays on the floor in an orange sweater as part of the Centerfold series.
Her Untitled #96 depicts herself lying in an orange shirt on a kitchen floor holding an ad.

works.name: Sex Pictures
works.description: Sherman's 1992 series using medical dolls, prosthetic limbs, and mannequin parts arranged into sexually explicit or pornographic positions, critiquing pornography. Created partly in the context of debates over NEA funding (Mapplethorpe, Serrano). Robert Mapplethorpe was cited as an influence. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Used medical dolls, prosthetic limbs, and mannequin parts in sexually explicit/pornographic arrangements to critique pornography
examples: This artist arranged artificial body parts into sexually explicit positions for the Sex Pictures series.

works.name: History Portraits
works.description: Sherman's series of self-portraits imitating Old Master paintings using costumes and prosthetics. Includes a reworking of Raphael's Fornarina (No. 205) and an imitation of Caravaggio's Young Sick Bacchus with fake grapes. Wikipedia

works.name: Clowns series
works.description: Sherman's series of self-portraits wearing clown outfits in front of digitally manipulated backgrounds. She digitally superimposed the title performers over each other. Wikipedia

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Cindy Sherman (born Cynthia Morris Sherman, 1954) is an American conceptual photographer associated with the Pictures Generation. Chuck Close called her 'perhaps the most important artist' of the 1980s. She is a MacArthur Fellow. She had a brief relationship with Robert Longo, who included her in his Men in the Cities series. She graduated from Buffalo State College. She created the Bus Riders series and recently posted self-portrait work on Instagram using Facetune. A 2016 Broad Museum exhibition titled 'The Imitation of Life' caused controversy when photos of Sherman in blackface surfaced. Suzy Lake is cited as a mutual influence; Sherman invited Lake to a 1975 exhibition at Hallwalls. Wikipedia

comprehensive_summary:
Cindy Sherman (born Cynthia Morris Sherman, 1954) is an American conceptual photographer associated with the Pictures Generation. Chuck Close called her 'perhaps the most important artist of the 1980s,' and she is a MacArthur Fellow.

Her best-known work is the Untitled Film Stills (1977–1980) — 69 black-and-white self-portraits in which Sherman poses as unnamed women in stereotypical female roles evocative of 1950s–60s Hollywood B-movies. Characters bear nicknames like 'The Hitchhiker' (Sherman next to a suitcase on an Arizona roadside) and 'City Girl' (Untitled #21, looking out at skyscrapers). All images are untitled. The photo Untitled #96 — Sherman lying on a kitchen floor in orange, clutching a crumpled newspaper ad — was for a time the second most expensive photograph ever sold at auction (before Andreas Gursky's Rhine II). Laura Mulvey's 1991 essay 'A Phantasmagoria of the Female Body' in New Left Review helped popularize the series. James Franco controversially attempted to reshoot the series with himself.

Sherman's Centerfolds (1981) subverted the magazine centerfold format with color photographs of women in distressed or pensive poses. The Sex Pictures (1992) used medical dolls, prosthetic limbs, and mannequin parts arranged in sexually explicit positions to critique pornography — created partly in the context of debates over NEA funding of Mapplethorpe and Andres Serrano. The History Portraits series imitated Old Master paintings through costume and prosthetics, including a reworking of Raphael's Fornarina (No. 205) and an imitation of Caravaggio's Young Sick Bacchus with fake grapes. The Clowns series showed Sherman in clown outfits against digitally manipulated backgrounds, with multiple clowns superimposed.

Sherman graduated from Buffalo State College and had a brief relationship with Robert Longo, who included her in his Men in the Cities series. In 2016, a Broad Museum exhibition titled 'The Imitation of Life' caused controversy when photographs of Sherman in blackface surfaced. Her recent work includes Instagram self-portraits manipulated with Facetune, created for W magazine. Suzy Lake is cited as a mutual influence; Sherman invited Lake to a 1975 exhibition at Hallwalls.

Work: self-portraits imitating Old Master paintings; includes reworkings of Raphael's Fornarina and Caravaggio's Young Sick Bacchus (with fake grapes)
Work: color photographs subverting the magazine centerfold format — showing women in distressed or pensive (rather than seductive) poses; commissioned by Artforum
Artist: Laura Mulvey's 1991 essay 'A Phantasmagoria of the Female Body' analyzed this photographer's work on stereotypical female imagery
Artist: had a brief relationship with Robert Longo, who included her in his Men in the Cities series
